turns.user_message: Honestly, I don't know why I'm here. I mean, I know I should quit, but it's just so hard. I've been smoking for 15 years, and it's just a part of my daily routine. I guess I'm just not sure if I'm ready to give it up.
step2_rag_chunks: If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.
Reminding yourself of the health benefits of quitting smoking can increase motivation.
Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the EXPLORATION phase. You know the basics. Now gently explore what situations or emotions make them smoke more — triggers like stress, meals, social settings, boredom. Do NOT suggest any strategies yet. Just understand their situation.

step6_prompt: You are a warm, empathetic therapist helping patients overcome tobacco addiction.

- Do NOT list techniques, bullet points, or dump information
- First acknowledge what the patient said, then ask ONE follow-up question
- Only suggest a technique when you are in the Planning phase
- Use simple, conversational language — not clinical or formal

turns.user_message: I don't know...I guess just not having that crutch anymore. And I don't really have anyone to support me. My friends all smoke, and I work from home, so I don't have anyone to hold me accountable.
step2_rag_chunks: Having even one person who knows you are trying to quit and supports you makes the process significantly easier. This is not about having someone watch over you â€” it is about having someone to call when the urge is strong, or someone who will not offer you a cigarette. Who in your life could be that person for you?
Reward yourself for milestones such as staying smoke-free for a day, a week, or a month.

step2_rag_chunks: Taking a short walk or doing light physical activity can help reduce stress and distract from cravings.
Talking to someone about your cravings can help reduce the intensity of the urge.
The 4D technique helps manage cravings: Delay smoking for a few minutes, take Deep breaths slowly, Drink water, and Do something else to distract yourself.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: The patient has signaled they are ready to act or is wrapping up. Your ONLY job is to give ONE warm closing sentence. Do NOT ask any question. One sentence only. Nothing more.
